---
title: "Lecture 31: Splines, LOESS, and Regularized Models"
subtitle: 161.251 Regression Modelling
author: "Presented by Nick Knowlton <N.Knowlton@massey.ac.nz>"
date: "Week 11 of Semester 2, `r lubridate::year(lubridate::now())`"
output:
  html_document:
    code_download: true
    theme: yeti
    highlight_style: pygments
  html_notebook:
    code_download: true
    theme: yeti
    highlight_style: pygments
  ioslides_presentation:
    widescreen: true
    smaller: true
  word_document: default
  slidy_presentation:
    theme: yeti
    highlight_style: pygments
  pdf_document: default
---




<!--- Data is on
https://r-resources.massey.ac.nz/data/161251/
--->

```{r setup, purl=FALSE, include=FALSE}
library(knitr)
opts_chunk$set(dev=c("png", "pdf"))
opts_chunk$set(fig.height=6, fig.width=7, fig.path="Figures/", fig.alt="unlabelled")
opts_chunk$set(comment="", fig.align="center", tidy=TRUE)
options(knitr.kable.NA = '')
library(tidyverse)
library(broom)
```


<!--- Do not edit anything above this line. --->

## Beyond Parametric Models

So far we have focused on parametric regression models — models where the form of the relationship between $Y$ and $X$ is specified by a small number of parameters (e.g. $\beta_0 + \beta_1 x$, or $\beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \beta_2 x^2$).

In this lecture we introduce three approaches that relax the strict parametric form:

- **Splines**: piecewise polynomials joined at knots
- **LOESS**: locally estimated scatterplot smoothing
- **Regularized models**: ridge regression and lasso

## Why Go Beyond Parametric Models?

- Parametric models impose a global structure on the data
- Real relationships may be more complex than a straight line or simple polynomial
- We may want to let the data "speak for themselves" without imposing a rigid functional form
- Regularization helps when we have many predictors relative to observations

## Splines

### What is a Spline?

A spline is a piecewise polynomial function that is smooth at the join points (knots).

- We already saw piecewise linear models in Lecture 18
- Splines generalize this to higher-degree polynomials with smoothness constraints
- Common types: linear splines, cubic splines, natural cubic splines, B-splines

### Cubic Splines

A cubic spline with $K$ knots at locations $\kappa_1, \ldots, \kappa_K$ is a piecewise cubic polynomial that is continuous and has continuous first and second derivatives at each knot.

```{r spline-example, eval=FALSE}
library(splines)
# Natural cubic spline with 3 degrees of freedom
model <- lm(y ~ ns(x, df = 3), data = mydata)
```

### Choosing Knots

- Number of knots controls flexibility (more knots = more wiggly)
- Placement: typically at quantiles of $x$
- Too many knots → overfitting; too few → underfitting
- Cross-validation can help choose the number of knots

## LOESS: Locally Estimated Scatterplot Smoothing

### The LOESS Idea

Instead of fitting one global model, LOESS fits many small local models:

1. For each point $x_0$ where we want a prediction, select a neighbourhood of nearby points
2. Fit a low-degree polynomial (usually linear or quadratic) using weighted least squares, where points closer to $x_0$ get more weight
3. The predicted value at $x_0$ is the fitted value from this local model

### LOESS in R

```{r loess-example, eval=FALSE}
# LOESS with span = 0.5 (50% of data used in each local fit)
loess_fit <- loess(y ~ x, data = mydata, span = 0.5)
# Predict on a grid
x_grid <- seq(min(x), max(x), length.out = 100)
y_pred <- predict(loess_fit, newdata = data.frame(x = x_grid))
```

### The Span Parameter

- `span` controls the fraction of data used in each local neighbourhood
- Small span → very local, wiggly fit (may overfit)
- Large span → smoother, more global fit (may underfit)
- Typical values: 0.2 to 0.8
- Can be chosen by cross-validation or AICc

### LOESS vs Splines

| Feature | LOESS | Splines |
|---------|-------|---------|
| Global model? | No (local fits) | Yes (global basis) |
| Prediction formula | No closed form | Yes (basis expansion) |
| Extrapolation | Poor | Better (with care) |
| Computation | $O(n^2)$ for prediction | $O(n)$ after fitting |
| Interpretability | Low | Moderate |

## Regularized Regression

### The Problem with Many Predictors

When we have many predictors (possibly more than observations), ordinary least squares:

- May have high variance
- May not have a unique solution (if $p > n$)
- Tends to overfit

### Ridge Regression

Ridge regression adds a penalty on the size of coefficients:

$$\hat{\beta}^{\text{ridge}} = \arg\min_\beta \left\{ \sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - x_i^T\beta)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^p \beta_j^2 \right\}$$

- Shrinks coefficients toward zero but never exactly to zero
- $\lambda$ controls the amount of shrinkage (chosen by cross-validation)
- Works well when many predictors each contribute a little

### Lasso

The lasso (Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator) uses an $L_1$ penalty:

$$\hat{\beta}^{\text{lasso}} = \arg\min_\beta \left\{ \sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - x_i^T\beta)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^p |\beta_j| \right\}$$

- Shrinks some coefficients exactly to zero → automatic variable selection
- Produces sparse models
- $\lambda$ chosen by cross-validation

### Ridge vs Lasso

```{r glmnet-example, eval=FALSE}
library(glmnet)
# Ridge (alpha = 0)
ridge_fit <- cv.glmnet(x, y, alpha = 0)
# Lasso (alpha = 1)
lasso_fit <- cv.glmnet(x, y, alpha = 1)
```

| Feature | Ridge | Lasso |
|---------|-------|-------|
| Penalty | $L_2$ (squared) | $L_1$ (absolute) |
| Coefficient shrinkage | Toward zero | Some to exactly zero |
| Variable selection | No | Yes |
| Correlated predictors | Shares coefficient | Picks one |
| When to use | Many small effects | Sparse true model |

## Summary

- **Splines** extend polynomial regression with piecewise fits and smoothness constraints
- **LOESS** provides flexible, assumption-light smoothing through local weighted regression
- **Ridge regression** shrinks coefficients to handle multicollinearity and high-dimensional data
- **Lasso** performs automatic variable selection by shrinking some coefficients to exactly zero

These methods bridge the gap between rigid parametric models and the need for flexible, data-driven modelling.
